text: HASTELLOY N AT 650 AND $760^{\circ}$ C
HASTELLOY N AT 650 AND $760^{\circ}C$
Creep Properties After 3000 hr of Aging 14
We have investigated the influence of small titanium additions on the thermal stability of Ni-12% Mo-7% Cr-0.07% C.

The mechanical properties at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (tensile tests at 0.002/min strain rate and creep tests at 40,000 psi stress) were measured for four heats of this alloy with titanium contents from 0.15 to 1.2%.
Solution annealing temperatures were 1177 or $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and subsequent precipitation heat treatments were conducted at 650 and $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

At low titanium levels the MC carbide is stable at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ but is unstable at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , where an $\mathbf{M}_2\mathbf{C}$ -type carbide is precipitated, resulting in inferior properties.
For the higher titanium concentrations the MC carbide is stable on aging at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and results in excellent properties after a solution anneal at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
However, high-titanium alloys are significantly less ductile if they are solution annealed at $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and aged at either 650 or $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The heat with the lowest carbon content $(0.04\%)$ was most resistant to property changes on aging up to 10,000 hr at both 650 and $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
We are concerned with modifying the composition of Hastelloy N for better performance as a material for a molten-salt thermal breeder reactor with an expected lifetime of 30 years or more.

The existing Hastelloy N developed primarily at Oak Ridge over the past 10 to 12 years functioned well in an 8-MW(t) reactor built five years ago and operated over the past three years.[2] However, this reactor operated at
$650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and when it was constructed very little was known about the problem of high-temperature irradiation damage to this alloy.

Since that time we have observed<sup>3</sup> damage by thermal neutrons on many materials, including nickel-base alloys such as Hastelloy N, in which a larger deterioration in properties is observed than in most iron-base alloys.

We found that small additions of reactive elements, notably titanium, can significantly enhance the postirradiation creep-rupture life and ductility.4 Thus, we have proceeded with the development of a modified Hastelloy N containing titanium.
These four 100-lb heats were vacuum induction melted with initial ingot breakdown at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and final fabrication to 0.5-in. plate at $870^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The titanium content was the primary variable, being 0.15, 0.27, 0.45, and $1.20\%$ as shown in Table 1.
Although a nominal carbon level of $0.07\%$ was specified, the carbon content of Heat 466-548 was lower than the others.
The various thermal and mechanical treatments applied are given in Table 2.

VariableLevels or Treatments
Titanium content, %0.15, 0.27, 0.45, and 1.2
Preage treatmentSolution anneal 1 hr at 1177°C
Solution anneal 1 hr at 1260°C

Anneal 1 hr at 1177°C, then prestrain 10% at room temperatureAging temperature, °C650 and 760Aging time, hr1500, 3000, and 10,000Testing after agingTensile650°C, 0.002/min strain rateCreep650°C, 40,000 psi stress
Two Chromel-P vs Alumel thermocouples were used with a proportioning controller to keep the test specimen at $650 \pm 3^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

In addition, precipitated phases present in certain alloys were identified by x-ray diffraction using the Debye-Scherrer technique on precipitates extracted electrolytically with a methanol-10% HCl solution.

Details of the microstructure examination have been reported, $^{5}$ and the alloying effects of titanium have been summarized. $^{6}$ We shall concern ourselves here with the detailed mechanical property response to heat treatment and a limited discussion of microstructures revealed in optical metallography.

Because of the large number of variables included in this study and the attempt to measure the complicated and very subtle differences introduced by titanium additions, these experiments were statistically designed as a full factorial replication<sup>7</sup> including the variables in Table 2.
Results of these statistical analyses have been discussed previously.<sup>8</sup>
Typical results of the influence of preage thermal-mechanical treatment, aging time, and temperature on the tensile and creep properties at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ will be presented first.

section: Effect of Preaging on the Mechanical Properties
text: Figure 2 shows the yield strength (0.2% offset) and total elongation values for the three preage treatments investigated.

section: Effect of Preaging on the Mechanical Properties
text: Solution annealing 1 hr at $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ lowered the strength and increased the ductility at each titanium level as compared to a 1-hr solution anneal at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

section: Effect of Preaging on the Mechanical Properties
text: Prestraining 10% at room temperature after a 1-hr $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ solution anneal doubled the high-temperature yield strength and reduced the ductility by one-third.

section: Effect of Preaging on the Mechanical Properties
text: Effect of Titanium Content and Preaging Treatments on the Strength and Ductility of Modified Hastelloy N at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
3 through 5 for both aging temperatures.

Aging at either 650 or $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ after a solution anneal at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ enhanced the ductility at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for the $1.2\%$ Ti heat and decreased it for the $0.15\%$ Ti heat.
After a 1-hr solution anneal at $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , the $1.2\%$ Ti heat lost ductility on aging at either aging temperature to the same extent as did the lowest titanium level, $0.15\%$ .

Thus, the larger grain size or greater amount of solute in solid solution after the $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ treatment made the high-titanium heat susceptible to a detrimental aging reaction to which it was immune after the $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ anneal.

Samples were solution annealed $1\mathrm{hr}$ at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , prestrained $10\%$ at room temperature, aged at either 650 or $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and then tested at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
3 for samples not prestrained; however, the overall ductility was lower for the prestrained material.
Effect of Titanium Content and Aging Time on the Tensile Ductility at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ after a 1-hr Solution Anneal at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Effect of Titanium Content and Aging Time on the Tensile Ductility at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ after a 1-hr Solution Anneal at $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Effect of Titanium Content and Aging Time on the Tensile Ductility at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ for Samples Solution Annealed 1 hr at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and Prestrained $10\%$ at Room Temperature.
For aging at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ the intermediate $(0.45\%)$ and high $(1.2\%)$ titanium heats showed an increase in ductility with aging time.
The low $(0.15\%)$ titanium heat exhibited a rapid loss in hot ductility with aging time at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Aging at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ affected the ductility of the low- and high-titanium heats the same as aging at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , whereas the intermediate $(0.45\%)$ titanium heat showed a ductility deterioration with time at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ in contrast to the ductility enhancement found after aging at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Aging 1500 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ increases yield strength more than at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
These trends in strength and ductility were found for heats 466-535, 466-541, and 466-548.
For heat 467-548 (1.2% Ti) we measured the effect of using a higher solution anneal temperature $(1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C})$ on the aging response.
Aging 1500 hr at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ increased the room-temperature yield strength from 36,000 to 60,000 psi and decreased the creep elongation from 68 to $42\%$ strain.
This change in the room-temperature tensile behavior for the $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ solution anneal was larger than that found after annealing at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
However, the microstructural differences found for this high-titanium (1.2% Ti, 467-548) heat are significant and have been discussed previously.

section: Creep Properties After 3000 hr of Aging
text: Creep Properties After 3000 hr of Aging

section: Creep Properties After 3000 hr of Aging
text: The effect of titanium content on the creep properties of solution-annealed and aged alloys was measured for 36 specimens.

section: Creep Properties After 3000 hr of Aging
text: Each test was conducted at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and under 40,000 psi stress so that the rupture lives and creep rates indicate the effects of aging.

section: Creep Properties After 3000 hr of Aging
text: 10 and 11 for some of the treatments used.

For each alloy, except the $1.2\%$ Ti heat, the lowest rupture life and highest elongation corresponded to samples solution annealed at the higher temperature (i.e., 1 hr at $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).
In most instances the lowest secondary creep rate, maximum rupture life, and minimum ductility corresponded to the samples prestrained $10\%$ at room temperature.
Variation in Rupture Life and Creep Rate with Titanium Content and Heat Treatment at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 40,000 psi Stress.
In most cases (10 of 12 tests) the rupture life after aging 3000 hr at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ was reduced by from 20 to $90\%$ as compared with the unaged samples.
Correspondingly, the creep rates were significantly increased by a factor of 3 to 10, depending on the alloy and heat treatment.
The creep ductility of the samples aged 3000 hr at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ranged from 6 to $37\%$ , with the higher values corresponding to the higher titanium level $(1.2\%)$ .
Less property deterioration was observed for aging 3000 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
In fact, several heats, notably the $0.45\%$ Ti heat, exhibited significantly enhanced rupture lives and fracture strains even though the creep rate was increased appreciably by the aging treatment.

In general, the prestrained samples and those with the larger grain size (i.e., solution annealed at $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ) showed the largest reduction in rupture life after aging at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Effect of Titanium Content and Heat Treatment on the 40,000-psi Creep Elongation at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
However, solution annealing for 1 hr at $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ led to creep property changes on aging that were quite significant for all levels of titanium.

section: Comparison of Tensile and Creep Results
text: Table 4 lists the heat treatments for each heat of material that corresponded to the maximum and minimum property value found in creep and tensile testing of the samples aged 3000 hr.

section: Comparison of Tensile and Creep Results
text: Since three treatments were included in this study [i.e., solution anneals of (1) 1 hr at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (2) $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and (3) 1 hr at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ plus $10\%$ prestrain at room temperature] the treatment that is not listed under either minimum or maximum in Table 4 would result in properties intermediate between the minimum and maximum values.

section: Comparison of Tensile and Creep Results
text: $^\mathrm{c}$ Creep properties measured at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and 40,000-psi stress.
In each case the maximum yield strength and maximum ultimate tensile strength at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ were found for the $10\%$ prestrained samples.
The minimum yield and ultimate strengths were found for the $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ treatment.
The minimum and maximum tensile ductilities corresponded to the strongest and weakest alloys in tensile tests, with one exception, the $0.45\%$ Ti heat.
Notably, the $1.2\%$ Ti heat exhibited the minimum and maximum creep resistance (as measured by the creep rate) for the 1177 and $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ anneals, respectively.
This indicates that creep of this higher titanium heat was affected more by solution annealing treatment than by $10\%$ cold working.
The minimum and maximum creep ductilities of the other alloys corresponded to the prestrained and to the $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ annealed material, respectively.
The prestrained material aged 3000 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ exhibited the maximum yield strength for each heat.
The minimum yield strength corresponded to either 1177 or $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ samples aged 3000 hr at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , depending on the alloy.
The maximum ultimate strength was for prestrained samples aged 3000 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ with one exception, the $0.15\%$ Ti heat.

The lowest ultimate strength (UTS) was found for the $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ treatment; at lower titanium level an aging treatment of 3000 hr at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ caused the lowest UTS, and at the higher titanium levels aging 3000 hr at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ caused the lowest UTS.
The rupture life was a maximum for prestrained samples aged at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and the treatment that produced the minimum rupture life differed for each alloy.

An interesting point is that the $0.15\%$ Ti heat gave both the maximum and the minimum rupture life for the larger grain size $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ treatment, depending on whether it was aged at 650 or $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Table 5 lists the phases identified by Debye-Scherrer x-ray diffraction analysis of electrolytically extracted precipitate particles after 3000 hr of aging at the indicated temperatures.
In the $0.15\%$ Ti heat a combination of MC and $\mathbf{M}_2\mathbf{C}$ type phases was present, but at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ only the $\mathbf{M}_2\mathbf{C}$ carbide was found.

At the intermediate titanium level $(0.45\%)$ only MC was present at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , while at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ a weak trace of MC and strong lines for $\mathbf{M}_2\mathbf{C}$ were found.
At the highest titanium level investigated $(1.20\%)$ the MC structure was present after aging at either temperature.

The carbides found in these alloys were complex compounds involving chromium, molybdenum, and titanium, and in several cases we found two different lattice parameters that generally fit the hexagonal $\mathbf{M}_2\mathbf{C}$ -type structure with the same c/a ratio of 1.63.
The effect of solution annealing at $1177$ or $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ on the grain size for three of the four alloys is shown in Fig.
Annealing 1 hr at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ did not produce a single-phase alloy for most of these heats.
In the alloys containing 0.15 and $1.2\%$ Ti after an $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ anneal, large stringers of second-phase particles were evident.
The 0.27 and $0.45\%$ Ti heats were virtually single phase after the $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ heat treatment.
After 1 hr at $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ the grain size of each heat was 2 to 4 times that found after the $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ anneal.
Also, fewer precipitate particles and stringers were present after 1 hr at $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
This figure compares the precipitate distributions for samples solution annealed at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and aged 10,000 hr at either 650 or $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
These photographs were taken near the fracture sites of tensile samples tested at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ after aging at the indicated temperatures.

After aging at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ the precipitate was generally finer, and the concentration of precipitate near grain boundaries appeared to be fairly heavy, particularly for the low-titanium alloy.
After aging 10,000 hr at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ the precipitate was rather coarse, and the concentration of precipitate near the grain boundary was lower than that found at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
The precipitate within the grain boundary was also coarse, as shown by the micrographs of the 0.27 and $0.45\%$ Ti heats in Fig.
Little precipitation occurred in the $1.2\%$ Ti heat, since the stringers evident in Fig.
13 were actually present to some extent even before aging; this was shown for the as-solution-annealed condition in Fig.
Microstructures Developed in Ni-12% Mo-7% Cr-0.07% C Alloys Modified with Titanium, Solution Annealed 1 hr at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , and Aged 10,000 hr.
Microstructures developed during aging 10,000 hr after a 1-hr solution anneal at $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ are shown in Fig.
These structures are typical also of those developed in 1500 and 3000 hr of aging and indicate a large variation in optical microstructure with titanium content.
The $1.2\%$ Ti alloy showed bands of precipitate stringers after aging at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Apparently, the stringers present initially along the hot-working direction of the rod stock were not completely eliminated even though the material was solution annealed at $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
In the 0.45 and $1.2\%$ Ti heats during aging at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , however, some additional precipitation occurred on both subgrain and grain boundaries.
The microstructures developed at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ after a $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ solution anneal, shown in Fig.
14, were actually quite similar to those found after the $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ anneal and $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ age (Fig.

however, the structure developed after a $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ anneal was much different from that developed in samples annealed at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

This "platelet"-type precipitate, which forms primarily after the $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ solution anneal and $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ age, has been shown<sup>10</sup> by transmission electron microscopy to result from precipitation on stacking faults.

15 for the 0.15 and $1.2\%$ Ti heats.

In samples aged at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , prestraining $10\%$ produced very little effect on the structure of the $0.15\%$ Ti heat, but the $1.2\%$ Ti heat showed much more precipitation in the prestrained samples (Fig.
15) than in samples aged without prestraining (Fig.
In the prestrained $1.2\%$ Ti alloy, both a fine random matrix precipitate and a crystallographic platelet-type precipitate are shown in Fig.
(the platelets are actually a precipitate on stacking faults, $^{11}$ as mentioned previously).

This apparent enhancement in formation of stacking fault precipitate produced by straining this $1.2\%$ Ti alloy before aging is consistent with the effect of prior straining on formation of stacking fault precipitates in niobium-doped stainless steels as reported by Silcock and Tunstall $^{11}$ and Naybour. $^{12}$ However, some differences in the effect of strain on the tendency to precipitate on stacking faults in steels and in this alloy are discussed in another paper. $^{13}$

section: Fracture of Samples Tested in Creep
text: Thus in the $0.45\%$ Ti heat, evidently either the type of precipitate present or its amount and distribution differed significantly between 1500 and 3000 hr of exposure at a temperature of $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ (phases were not identified in the samples aged 1500 hr).

We have investigated the effects of titanium content, solution annealing temperature, aging temperature, and aging time on the mechanical properties of modified Hastelloy N at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
In the solution-annealed condition the 0.002/min yield strength increased and the ductility was approximately constant with increasing titanium content from 0.15 to $1.2\%$ .
Aging and prestraining both generally increased the yield strength, but the changes in ductility during aging depended on (1) alloy content, (2) annealing temperature, and (3) aging temperature.
An $\mathrm{M}_2\mathrm{C}$ -type was favored in heats with low titanium contents or alloys aged at the higher temperature, $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
An MC-type carbide was favored at higher titanium contents or for alloys with a given titanium concentration aged at the lower temperature, $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .
Correlations of microstructures and high-temperature mechanical properties as influenced by the carbon and titanium contents have previously been investigated.[14]
This was indicated by the $1.2\%$ Ti alloy, which lost ductility and increased its strength on aging after the $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ solution anneal.
Correlations of microstructures and mechanical properties for stacking fault precipitates have also been made for stainless steels, $^{15,16}$ Inconel, $^{17}$ and Hastelloy N. $^{18,19}$
The alloy with the lowest carbon content (i.e., heat 466-548) generally had the lowest strength and highest ductility.

This low carbon alloy, however, did show a pronounced difference in aging response at 650 and $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , which we attribute to the fact that different carbides were precipitated at the two aging temperatures (i.e., MC at $650^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ and $\mathbf{M}_2\mathbf{C}$ at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ ).
Overaging could be expected in most of these alloys before 3000 hr at $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ , as indicated from the peak in yield strength at 1500 hr in Fig.
626-630 in Second International Conference on the Strength of Metals and Alloys, Conf.
II, The American Society for Metals, Metals Park, Ohio, 1970.

The weakest alloys in creep (as measured by the highest secondary creep rate) were found for the $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ anneal, which produces a smaller grain size relative to the structure produced at $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ .

Of the treatments investigated, cold working $10\%$ at room temperature after a 1-hr solution anneal at $1177^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ had the greatest effect on raising the strength and lowering the ductility, both before and after aging.

An exception to this was found for the $1.2\%$ Ti heat, which exhibited the lowest post-age tensile and creep ductility after a $1260^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ solution anneal and $760^{\circ}\mathrm{C}$ age.

The heat treatments investigated produced variations in the high-temperature ductility between 5 and $50\%$ , yet the significant point is that most of these treatments change the room-temperature properties only slightly.
